texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Dynamics of a Binary Option Market with Exogenous Information and Price Sensitivity
texts.text: Dynamics of a Binary Option Market with Exogenous Information and Price Sensitivity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hannah Gampe 1, ∗ and Christopher Griffin 1, †
texts.text: Hannah Gampe 1, ∗ and Christopher Griffin 1, †

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1 Applied Research Laboratory, Penn State University, University Park, PA 16802
texts.text: 1 Applied Research Laboratory, Penn State University, University Park, PA 16802

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Dated: May 17, 2022)
texts.text: (Dated: May 17, 2022)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we derive and analyze a continuous of a binary option market with exogenous information. The resulting non-linear system has a discontinuous right hand side, which can be analyzed using zero-dimensional Filippov surfaces. Under general assumptions on purchasing rules, we show that when exogenous information is constant in the binary asset market, the price always converges. We then investigate market prices in the case of changing information, showing empirically that price sensitivity has a strong effect on price lag vs. information. We conclude with open questions on general n -ary option markets. As a by-product of the analysis, we show that these markets are equivalent to a simple recurrent neural network, helping to explain some of the predictive power associated with prediction markets, which are usually designed as n -ary option markets.
texts.text: In this paper, we derive and analyze a continuous of a binary option market with exogenous information. The resulting non-linear system has a discontinuous right hand side, which can be analyzed using zero-dimensional Filippov surfaces. Under general assumptions on purchasing rules, we show that when exogenous information is constant in the binary asset market, the price always converges. We then investigate market prices in the case of changing information, showing empirically that price sensitivity has a strong effect on price lag vs. information. We conclude with open questions on general n -ary option markets. As a by-product of the analysis, we show that these markets are equivalent to a simple recurrent neural network, helping to explain some of the predictive power associated with prediction markets, which are usually designed as n -ary option markets.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Market models have been studied extensively both through simulation and in theory in the econophysics literature [1-10]. In contrast to traditional stock or bond markets, prediction markets have assets corresponding to future events (e.g., elections [11], sports outcomes [12] etc.) that can be bought and sold causing changes in the underlying asset prices. Asset prices may be interpreted as probabilities [13, 14]. Prediction markets were first studied by Hanson [15-18]. Since this initial work, they have been studied extensively [13, 14, 19-23]. For a survey of work in this area through 2007 see [24]. These prediction markets are archetypical examples of binary option markets (in the two outcome case) [25]. Their use on Wall Street dates to the 1800's [26]. Artificial prediction markets were first explored by Chen et al. [27-29]. This work shows a connection between learning and the prediction market showing that if a prediction market has a cost function with bounded loss, then it has an interpretation as a no-regret learning algorithm [28]. This work is generalized in [29]. In the finance literature, binary options are usually used as a theoretical construct since they have a particularly simple Black-Scholes formulation [30].
texts.text: Market models have been studied extensively both through simulation and in theory in the econophysics literature [1-10]. In contrast to traditional stock or bond markets, prediction markets have assets corresponding to future events (e.g., elections [11], sports outcomes [12] etc.) that can be bought and sold causing changes in the underlying asset prices. Asset prices may be interpreted as probabilities [13, 14]. Prediction markets were first studied by Hanson [15-18]. Since this initial work, they have been studied extensively [13, 14, 19-23]. For a survey of work in this area through 2007 see [24]. These prediction markets are archetypical examples of binary option markets (in the two outcome case) [25]. Their use on Wall Street dates to the 1800's [26]. Artificial prediction markets were first explored by Chen et al. [27-29]. This work shows a connection between learning and the prediction market showing that if a prediction market has a cost function with bounded loss, then it has an interpretation as a no-regret learning algorithm [28]. This work is generalized in [29]. In the finance literature, binary options are usually used as a theoretical construct since they have a particularly simple Black-Scholes formulation [30].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we study the potential dynamics of prediction markets under continuum limits. In particular, we generalize and then extend the market model described in [31] to a continuous time dynamic. The results of this paper are:
texts.text: In this paper, we study the potential dynamics of prediction markets under continuum limits. In particular, we generalize and then extend the market model described in [31] to a continuous time dynamic. The results of this paper are:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. We show that under a constant information assumption, all asset prices converge. This explains empirical results found in [31]. The result follows from an argument on Filippov surfaces.
texts.text: We show that under a constant information assumption, all asset prices converge. This explains empirical results found in [31]. The result follows from an argument on Filippov surfaces.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. We then study the impact of dynamic information on market dynamics and empirically quantify the effect of price sensitivity and on lag between the market price and the information signal.
texts.text: We then study the impact of dynamic information on market dynamics and empirically quantify the effect of price sensitivity and on lag between the market price and the information signal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows: We show the model derivation in Section II. In Section III we show that with constant information, all binary prediction markets converge to a constant price. In Section IV, we study the numerical results of a non-constant information. We conclude and present future directions in Section VI.
texts.text: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows: We show the model derivation in Section II. In Section III we show that with constant information, all binary prediction markets converge to a constant price. In Section IV, we study the numerical results of a non-constant information. We conclude and present future directions in Section VI.

texts.orig: A. Agent Purchase Logic and Information

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following [31], we assume agents specialize in the purchase of a specific asset class (either 0 or 1) and that there is some (possibly time-varying) external information x ( t ) ∈ R n that will inform this purchase decision.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: [31] assumes that x is a vector in an appropriate feature space and for some scalar R proposes that ψ ( x , p ) ≥ R should define a convex region in R n × R 2 . Thus, a collection of (price sensitive) agents encode a set of time-varying sets in feature space and an agent purchases a share of a relevant asset if and only if the information encoded in the (constant) feature and price vector ( x , p ) is an element of the region defined by σ [ ψ ( x , p )]. [31] shows that price insensitive agents can be used to define an arbitrary covering region to encode a binary classification problem but observes in discrete time that minor oscillations can occur in the price. We show that this cannot happen in continuous time assuming a constant feature vector and then explore the affects of a non-constant feature vector.
texts.text: [31] assumes that x is a vector in an appropriate feature space and for some scalar R proposes that ψ ( x , p ) ≥ R should define a convex region in R n × R 2 . Thus, a collection of (price sensitive) agents encode a set of time-varying sets in feature space and an agent purchases a share of a relevant asset if and only if the information encoded in the (constant) feature and price vector ( x , p ) is an element of the region defined by σ [ ψ ( x , p )]. [31] shows that price insensitive agents can be used to define an arbitrary covering region to encode a binary classification problem but observes in discrete time that minor oscillations can occur in the price. We show that this cannot happen in continuous time assuming a constant feature vector and then explore the affects of a non-constant feature vector.

texts.text: The right-hand-side is piecewise smooth and bounded and therefore a solution to this ODE exists [36]. It is clear that p ∗ = 0 and p ∗ = 1 are fixed points of Eq. (8). Therefore, for p 0 ∈ [0 , 1], if Eq. (7) if p ( t ) is a solution, then p ( t ) ∈ [0 , 1] for all time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have just shown that the dynamics of p ( t ) are contained entirely in the smooth manifold M = [0 , 1]. Thus the flow at any point M is defined by a scalar (since the dimension of M is 1). By convention positive flow moves toward p ∗ = 1. A sliding mode fixed point or fixed point on a Filippov surface is a point p ∗ ∈ int( M ) (a zero dimensional manifold) where the flow to the left of p ∗ is positive and the flow to the right of p ∗ is negative and the flow at p ∗ may take either a positive or negative or zero value [36]. That is, the flow around this point is moving in opposite directions. Naturally, this definition can be extended to higher dimensional flows (see [36]) but we do not require this level of complexity.
texts.text: We have just shown that the dynamics of p ( t ) are contained entirely in the smooth manifold M = [0 , 1]. Thus the flow at any point M is defined by a scalar (since the dimension of M is 1). By convention positive flow moves toward p ∗ = 1. A sliding mode fixed point or fixed point on a Filippov surface is a point p ∗ ∈ int( M ) (a zero dimensional manifold) where the flow to the left of p ∗ is positive and the flow to the right of p ∗ is negative and the flow at p ∗ may take either a positive or negative or zero value [36]. That is, the flow around this point is moving in opposite directions. Naturally, this definition can be extended to higher dimensional flows (see [36]) but we do not require this level of complexity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If N ( p 0 ) = 0, then p = p 0 is the solution for all time. On the other hand, suppose N ( p 0 ) < 0, then p ( t ) is initially decreasing and we have three possibilities.
texts.text: If N ( p 0 ) = 0, then p = p 0 is the solution for all time. On the other hand, suppose N ( p 0 ) < 0, then p ( t ) is initially decreasing and we have three possibilities.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Case I: If N [ p ( t )] remains negative as p ( t ) decreases, then p ( t ) decreases for all time, asymptotically approaching the fixed point p ∗ = 0.
texts.text: Case I: If N [ p ( t )] remains negative as p ( t ) decreases, then p ( t ) decreases for all time, asymptotically approaching the fixed point p ∗ = 0.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Case II: If N [ p ( t )] increases as p ( t ) decreases and there is a finite t ∗ > 0 such that N [ p ( t ∗ )] = 0, then for all t ≥ t ∗ , p ( t ) = p ( t ∗ ).
texts.text: Case II: If N [ p ( t )] increases as p ( t ) decreases and there is a finite t ∗ > 0 such that N [ p ( t ∗ )] = 0, then for all t ≥ t ∗ , p ( t ) = p ( t ∗ ).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Case III: If N [ p ( t )] increases as p ( t ) decreases and there is a smallest time t ∗ > 0 so that for all ϵ > 0, N [ p ( t ∗ + ϵ )] > 0 and N [ p ( t ∗ -ϵ )] < 0, then p ( t ∗ ) is a sliding mode fixed point. Consequently, the value of p ( t ) becomes fixed at the location of the jump discontinuity in N ( p ). This is illustrated in Fig. 2.
texts.text: Case III: If N [ p ( t )] increases as p ( t ) decreases and there is a smallest time t ∗ > 0 so that for all ϵ > 0, N [ p ( t ∗ + ϵ )] > 0 and N [ p ( t ∗ -ϵ )] < 0, then p ( t ∗ ) is a sliding mode fixed point. Consequently, the value of p ( t ) becomes fixed at the location of the jump discontinuity in N ( p ). This is illustrated in Fig. 2.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: FIG. 2: Illustration of a function n ( p ) with a jump discontinuity and the resulting convergence to the sliding mode manifold.
texts.text: FIG. 2: Illustration of a function n ( p ) with a jump discontinuity and the resulting convergence to the sliding mode manifold.

texts.orig: The argument for the case when N ( p 0 ) > 0 is symmetric. Thus, we have shown that: (i) Every instance of Eq. (7) has a global continuous and piecewise smooth solution. (ii) Solutions are monotonic. (iii) Every solution either asymptotically approach p ∗ = 0 or p ∗ = 1 or reaches a constant value infinite time.
texts.text: The argument for the case when N ( p 0 ) > 0 is symmetric. Thus, we have shown that: (i) Every instance of Eq. (7) has a global continuous and piecewise smooth solution. (ii) Solutions are monotonic. (iii) Every solution either asymptotically approach p ∗ = 0 or p ∗ = 1 or reaches a constant value infinite time.

The market with smaller numbers of agents tracks the information but there are times when x ( t ) is not in any interval defining an agent and so buying stops. This leads to plateaus in the data.

The market with smaller numbers of agents tracks the information but there are times when x ( t ) is not in any interval defining an agent and so buying stops. This leads to plateaus in the data.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: we can examine the effect asset price sensitivity ( ν ) and input gain ( α ) have on the lag in information uptake in the market. To do this, we use the measured phase difference between p ( t ) and x ( t ) using the argument of the Fourier transform of the solutions at the fundamental frequency ω . The ratio of the two phases provides a measure of the speed with which information is taken up by the market. We used markets with 250 agents (125 agents per class) and allows α to vary from 1 to 15 and ν to vary in the set {-1 , 0 , 1 } . For all experiments β = 0 . 01. When ν = 0, there is no price sensitivity. When ν = -1, agents are more sensitive to price and higher prices make them value the asset lower. Effectively, this creates additional friction in purchasing. When ν = 1, agents are price sensitive and higher prices causes them to value an asset more. This creates additional force in the the market to purchase even at higher prices. We used 5 replications of each condition to construct confidence intervals on the mean of the phase ratios. Agent intervals were randomized in each replication. Throughout all experiments, x ( t ) was held constant with A = 1 and ω = 2 π/ 25000. Results are shown in Fig. 5. We conclude that agents with positive price sensitivity resulted in a higher phase ratio while agents with a negative price sensitivity had a lesser phase ratio for varying input gain ( α ). Price insensitive agents' phase ratio fell between the positive and negative price sensitive agents. Hence, when ν = -1, information is taken up by the market faster than when ν = 0 and when ν = 1. We also note that the phase ratio appears to exhibit saturation in the positive price sensitive agents, which should be expected.

The ratio of the two phases provides a measure of the speed with which information is taken up by the market. When ν = 0, there is no price sensitivity. When ν = -1, agents are more sensitive to price and higher prices make them value the asset lower. Effectively, this creates additional friction in purchasing. When ν = 1, agents are price sensitive and higher prices causes them to value an asset more. This creates additional force in the the market to purchase even at higher prices. We used 5 replications of each condition to construct confidence intervals on the mean of the phase ratios. Agent intervals were randomized in each replication. Throughout all experiments, x ( t ) was held constant with A = 1 and ω = 2 π/ 25000. Results are shown in Fig. 5. We conclude that agents with positive price sensitivity resulted in a higher phase ratio while agents with a negative price sensitivity had a lesser phase ratio for varying input gain ( α ). Price insensitive agents' phase ratio fell between the positive and negative price sensitive agents. Hence, when ν = -1, information is taken up by the market faster than when ν = 0 and when ν = 1. We also note that the phase ratio appears to exhibit saturation in the positive price sensitive agents, which should be expected.

texts.orig: V. GENERALIZATION AND CONJECTURE
texts.text: V. GENERALIZATION AND CONJECTURE

The dynamics effectively describe a recurrent neural network with an embedded softmax layer (generalizing Fig. 1). We conjecture that with constant information, the resulting dynamics always converge, fully generalizing the result in Section III. We illustrate this conjecture in Fig. 6 for a three asset market simulated with over 3000 variations on the dynamics assuming:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In particular, our conjecture implies that the dynamics quickly reach an k < M dimensional Filippov surface and then slide along it until they reach a fixed point (a zero dimensional subset of the Filippov surface) thus ensuring convergence of the system. Assuming this conjecture is correct, then synthetic prediction markets (of the type discussed in [31]) can be extended to arbitrary prediction classes and the use of continuous time markets will eliminate oscillations observed in the discrete time algorithm. In addition to the open question of the convergence of M -array option markets with M > 2, we leave as an area of future research how best to design functions σ and ψ i ( i = 1 , . . . , N ) to recover desired market output values given input data { x j } j .
texts.text: In particular, our conjecture implies that the dynamics quickly reach an k < M dimensional Filippov surface and then slide along it until they reach a fixed point (a zero dimensional subset of the Filippov surface) thus ensuring convergence of the system. Assuming this conjecture is correct, then synthetic prediction markets (of the type discussed in [31]) can be extended to arbitrary prediction classes and the use of continuous time markets will eliminate oscillations observed in the discrete time algorithm. In addition to the open question of the convergence of M -array option markets with M > 2, we leave as an area of future research how best to design functions σ and ψ i ( i = 1 , . . . , N ) to recover desired market output values given input data { x j } j .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: VI. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS
texts.text: VI. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we have prove the convergence of price in a continuous binary option market with constant external information. This is proved by showing that the right-handside of the dynamics are piecewise smooth and bounded allowing us to reason about the Filippov surface on which the dynamics come to rest. Consequently, the price dynamics are always monotonic, continuous, and piecewise smooth and always converge. In the presence of non-constant external information, we show empirically that a positive price sensitivity (i.e., purchases increase in response to higher prices) causes larger market lags to external information change while negative price sensitivity (i.e., purchases decrease in response to higher prices) decrease market lag to external information.
texts.text: In this paper, we have prove the convergence of price in a continuous binary option market with constant external information. This is proved by showing that the right-handside of the dynamics are piecewise smooth and bounded allowing us to reason about the Filippov surface on which the dynamics come to rest. Consequently, the price dynamics are always monotonic, continuous, and piecewise smooth and always converge. In the presence of non-constant external information, we show empirically that a positive price sensitivity (i.e., purchases increase in response to higher prices) causes larger market lags to external information change while negative price sensitivity (i.e., purchases decrease in response to higher prices) decrease market lag to external information.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results discussed in this paper can be extended in several ways. We have left an open conjecture on the generalization of the market convergence result to markets with M > 2 asset classes. We assert that under constant external information these markets also converge. If this is true, then the use of continuous time M -array asset markets as an approach to machine learning would be sensible. In this case, since we have shown M -array option markets describe a recurrent neural network, investigating how to fit exogenous parameters defining the functions φ i to build a classifier is an interesting and open question, especially considering the recurrent aspect of the neural structure. Finally further analysis in the case when the exogenous signal x ( t ) is non-constant may provide further insight into the behavior of these simple options markets.
texts.text: Results discussed in this paper can be extended in several ways. We have left an open conjecture on the generalization of the market convergence result to markets with M > 2 asset classes. We assert that under constant external information these markets also converge. If this is true, then the use of continuous time M -array asset markets as an approach to machine learning would be sensible. In this case, since we have shown M -array option markets describe a recurrent neural network, investigating how to fit exogenous parameters defining the functions φ i to build a classifier is an interesting and open question, especially considering the recurrent aspect of the neural structure. Finally further analysis in the case when the exogenous signal x ( t ) is non-constant may provide further insight into the behavior of these simple options markets.

texts.text: Appendix B: Numerical Solution of Large Differential Equations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When x ( t ) is complex or there are more than a few agents, numerical solution of the differential equations can become unstable. To compensate for this, we can solve Eqs. (5) and (6) using a simple Euler forward step method using a small ϵ in Eq. (4) (see below):
texts.text: When x ( t ) is complex or there are more than a few agents, numerical solution of the differential equations can become unstable. To compensate for this, we can solve Eqs. (5) and (6) using a simple Euler forward step method using a small ϵ in Eq. (4) (see below):

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We then compute p ( t ) using information for q 1 ( t ) and q 0 ( t ) up to time t via Eq. (2). For small ϵ ≈ 0 . 01, the results match the output of an off-the-shelf ODE solver and are more numerically stable.
texts.text: We then compute p ( t ) using information for q 1 ( t ) and q 0 ( t ) up to time t via Eq. (2). For small ϵ ≈ 0 . 01, the results match the output of an off-the-shelf ODE solver and are more numerically stable.
